title: Share of the population in poverty vs. Electoral Democracy Index
subtitle: Poverty is defined as living on less than $10 a day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. The electoral democracy index is by [V-Dem](#dod:v-dem), based on expert estimates, and ranges from 0 to 1 (most democratic).
caption:
Share of the population in poverty vs. Electoral Democracy Index, 2025

Poverty is defined as living on less than $10 a day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. The electoral democracy index is by V-Dem, based on expert estimates, and ranges from 0 to 1 (most democratic).

The scatter plot positions countries by their electoral democracy index on the horizontal axis and the share of the population living in poverty on the vertical axis, with data expressed in international dollars at 2021 prices. Points are color-coded by region, covering North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The distribution shows a negative relationship where higher democracy scores generally coincide with lower poverty rates. In the upper-left quadrant, countries with low democracy scores and high poverty include Burundi, Ethiopia, and Benin, where poverty rates exceed 80 percent. Laos and Egypt also appear in this cluster with poverty rates between 70 and 80 percent and democracy scores below 0.2. Moving toward the center, nations like Indonesia, Georgia, and Armenia display moderate democracy scores around 0.5 to 0.6 with poverty rates ranging from 60 to 70 percent. South American countries such as Peru, Colombia, and Brazil occupy the middle-right section, showing democracy scores between 0.6 and 0.8 and poverty rates between 20 and 50 percent. At the far right, countries with the highest democracy scores, including Australia, Costa Rica, and Chile, cluster near the bottom axis with poverty rates below 20 percent. European nations like Bulgaria and Moldova also appear in the lower-right area with high democracy scores and poverty rates under 40 percent. The data points form a downward slope from left to right, indicating that countries with the lowest electoral democracy index values tend to have the highest shares of their populations living in poverty.
